back: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical (7 → 1).

front: Common ports: HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, DNS
back: HTTP 80/TCP, HTTPS 443/TCP, SSH 22/TCP, DNS 53/UDP+TCP.
TCP: connection-oriented, reliable, ordered. UDP: connectionless, low-overhead, good for real-time.
IPv4 is 32-bit (dotted decimal); IPv6 is 128-bit (hex groups).
The router a host sends traffic to when the destination is outside its local subnet.

front: DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX
back: A → IPv4, AAAA → IPv6, CNAME → alias, MX → mail server.
Server UDP 67, client UDP 68. (Also remember NTP 123, SMTP 25.)
6 host bits = 64 addresses − 2 = 62 usable hosts.
Tags Ethernet frames with a VLAN ID so trunk links can carry multiple VLANs between switches.
~100 meters per run; beyond that, use fiber.
2.4 GHz: longer range, more interference, fewer channels. 5 GHz: shorter range, faster, more non-overlapping channels.
Straight-through: unlike devices (PC↔switch). Crossover: like devices (switch↔switch), pre-auto-MDI-X.
802.3af / 802.3at deliver power over the data cable to APs, phones, and cameras.
WEP and WPA are deprecated; WPA2 is common; WPA3 is the most secure current choice.
Prevents switching loops by blocking redundant paths until needed.
Maps many private addresses to one/few public IPs, conserving IPv4 and hiding internal addressing.
Static: manual, no adaptation (small nets). Dynamic (e.g., OSPF): learns and reconverges automatically.
RTO = target time to restore service. RPO = maximum acceptable data loss measured in time.
Polling device metrics and receiving trap alerts from network devices into a monitoring system.
HA removes single points of failure (failover); load balancing spreads traffic across multiple active resources.
A protocol that centralizes log messages from many devices to one collector for review and retention.
Summarizes conversations (src/dst, ports, volume) to find top talkers and analyze bandwidth use.
Service Level Agreement — committed, measurable service levels such as uptime and response time.

front: Hot vs warm vs cold site
back: Hot: ready with current data (minutes). Warm: partially ready (hours/days). Cold: space and power only.
To restore a known-good configuration quickly after hardware failure or a bad change.
Network Time Protocol (UDP 123) keeps device clocks in sync — vital for logs and authentication.
Confidentiality (secrecy), Integrity (no unauthorized change), Availability (reachable when needed).
Forged ARP replies bind the attacker's MAC to another IP, enabling on-path interception. Mitigate with Dynamic ARP Inspection.
Port-based network access control: authenticate (often via RADIUS) before the switch port grants access.
Firewall posture that blocks all traffic not explicitly allowed, shrinking the attack surface.
An isolated segment for internet-facing servers so their compromise doesn't expose the internal LAN.
Limits which/how many MAC addresses a switch port accepts; can disable the port on a violation.
Trusts only approved ports for DHCP server messages, blocking rogue DHCP servers.
To limit lateral movement so a breach in one zone doesn't expose the whole network.
Encrypted tunnel (IPsec or TLS) for secure remote access across untrusted networks.
Identify problem → theory of cause → test theory → plan → implement → verify → document.

front: Works by IP but not by name
back: Name-resolution (DNS) problem. Check the client's DNS server and test with nslookup/dig.

front: CRC errors on a port indicate…
back: Physical-layer corruption — damaged/out-of-spec cabling, excessive length, or interference.
ping tests reachability/latency to one host; traceroute lists each hop and where the path breaks.
Shows a host's IP address, subnet mask, and default gateway.
Suspect a wrong or missing default gateway — traffic can't leave the local subnet.
127.0.0.1 — pinging it confirms the local TCP/IP stack works.
Check latency, jitter, and packet loss — real-time media is sensitive to these, not just bandwidth.
Two devices share an address, often a static IP set inside the DHCP range. Reserve statics outside the scope.
